text: The surgeon observes the appendix positioned within the amiant hernia, indicating its unusual location.
The surgeon works laterally, sweeping tissues down and away, moving back and forth to connect the dissection planes.
The surgeon navigates the surgical field, focusing on the posterior rectus sheath, which is visible as it comes into view. The suprapubic space appears frozen due to previous prostatectomy.
The surgeon attempts to come down directly onto the sac, sweeping surrounding tissues laterally to expose and isolate the structure.
The surgeon encounters sticky tissue while carefully isolating anatomical landmarks, proceeding slowly to navigate the surgical field.

The surgeon pulls the bladder back towards themselves, using a technique that involves pulling out of the trocar to detach it from the pubic bone. The iliopubic tract is approached during this maneuver.
The surgeon observes the appendix still adhered in the surgical field and continues to sweep tissue downwards, staying close to the sac while manipulating the surrounding structures.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the appendix, which is positioned below, while working directly on the sac, ensuring precise dissection in the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the sac, revealing the appendix positioned closely underneath. The surgeon gently pulls up and twists the sac to encourage the appendix to fall away, bringing it into view.
The surgeon sweeps the cord structures down off the hernia sac, revealing the appendix being pulled upward. The appendix appears fused to the hernia sac, prompting further investigation.

The surgeon carefully pulls the appendix laterally to avoid disrupting its blood supply, visually establishing the path of blood flow to the appendix while maintaining a safe distance from critical structures.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the spermatic cord, ensuring it remains intact while addressing the medial edge. The area is approached with precision to avoid recurrence, especially challenging due to the patient's previous prostate and lymph node dissection.

The surgeon isolates the spermatic cord, performing gentle sweeps and applying cautery to separate the surrounding tissue. The areolar tissue becomes visible as it is pulled away during the dissection.
The surgeon works higher in the surgical field than initially anticipated, ensuring adequate space for mesh placement. The flap is pushed down to create a smooth area for the mesh to lay perfectly.
The surgeon ensures the mesh is positioned correctly, preserving the blood supply while planning the closure. The mesh is sutured in a standard fashion.
The surgeon completes a stitch, running it medially, while addressing the appendix and lower flap. The top flap is initially managed before returning to the lower flap to correct any deformities.
The surgeon splits the flap down the middle of the mesh, extending the incision towards the epigastric vessels. Reconstruction is performed using a V-lock suture, working from the top down.

The surgeon cinches the mesh, causing it to expose itself and hold up in the surgical field. The mesh is anchored superiorly, and as it is progressively closed, it is lifted from the lower pelvic region.

The surgeon focuses on the appendix while cinching up thickened flaps and hernia sacs, using bipolar cautery on vessels within the robust blood supply. The mesoappendix boundary is unclear, prompting skiving of the flap and peritoneal closure to cinch up the tissue. A visible hole is noted, large enough for an interparietal hernia.
